vix_stress.headline: VIX 18.43 versus OVX 72.35 leaves a 53.92-point spread, so equity calm still understates commodity execution friction for soybean-oil buyers.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Volatility pressure rose to 47.1 because VIX closed at 18.43 while OVX held at 72.35, keeping commodity-specific slippage risk materially above the broad-market fear read.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This is 5.8 points above the 41.3 average network pressure, so volatility is back in the watch tier even though it is not the lead driver.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: The transmission here is execution quality, not physical oil scarcity. Buyers can still source product, but price discovery gets less forgiving when OVX remains above 70.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Commodity volatility is still more sensitive than equity volatility, which means energy and policy shocks can hit procurement timing before recession-style fear appears in the broad tape.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: CFTC still shows managed money net long 158,102 contracts, or 22.45% of open interest, so a volatility flare can still accelerate upside cost reflexes.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Keep this in the watch column unless VIX pushes through 20 while OVX stays above 70; that would convert friction into a broader stress confirmation.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris should keep layered execution discipline. The tape is not panic-level, but it is too sticky for one-window complacency.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Detect when commodity volatility is becoming procurement-disruptive before a cost spike fully prints into soybean oil replacement pricing.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A low-VIX but still-elevated OVX regime means soybean-oil buyers can face commodity-specific execution damage before broader macro fear confirms the move.
quantResearchProtocol: Track VIX, OVX, and the OVX minus VIX spread together rather than in isolation.
Flag frictional risk whenever OVX stays above 65 even if VIX is sub-20.
Escalate from friction to disruption only when both volatility complexes start rising together.
Translate the signal into fill-quality and timing-risk language instead of generic volatility commentary.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call this a panic regime while VIX remains below 20.
Do not dismiss commodity stress simply because equities are calm.
Do not present volatility as directional price certainty without linking it to buyer execution risk.
outputRequirements: Quote VIX, OVX, and the spread explicitly.
State whether the regime is calm, frictional, or disruptive.
Give the buyer timing consequence in plain operational language.

notes: Spread-based framing separates broad calm from commodity-specific execution friction.
Narrative stays buyer-side and timing-focused rather than trader-side.

crush_pressure.headline: Board crush is 3.545 and oil share is 52.86%, so processor economics still support throughput even with a 0.54-point five-session oil-leg fade.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Crush pressure is only 15.8; positive crush math and a still-majority oil share say processors are not under the type of margin compression that usually tightens nearby soybean-oil availability.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This sits 25.5 points below the 41.3 average network pressure, making it the calmest channel in the stack.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: Oil share slipped, but the combined margin stack still argues for throughput continuity rather than plant-curtailment risk.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Trade or biofuel headlines could reprice this channel fast, but today the economics do not support a scarcity narrative on their own.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: Because speculative length is still bullish, any future squeeze in processor economics could matter quickly, but the current math is not there yet.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Escalate only if board crush compresses and oil share keeps falling together. Right now the oil-share drift is yellow, not red.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris should monitor the oil leg as an early-warning variable, but current crush math does not justify panic buying.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Separate processor-economics watch signals from true near-term availability stress before soybean-oil buyers overreact.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Buyer pain arrives when board crush and oil share deteriorate together; either metric alone can misclassify the procurement state.
quantResearchProtocol: Compute board crush from beans, meal, and oil every refresh.
Track oil-share level and five-session drift alongside the crush margin.
Classify the channel as supportive, compressing, or margin-risk only when both variables align.
Translate the output into buyer pacing instead of generic processor commentary.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call scarcity while board crush remains positive and stable.
Do not treat an oil-share fade by itself as proof of supply stress.
Do not blur profitability risk with physical availability risk.
outputRequirements: Report board crush, oil share, and the short-window oil-share delta.
State whether the channel is margin compression or throughput support.
Tie the conclusion directly to buyer pacing.

sourceRecords.recordHint: ZL closes used in oil-leg valuation
ZM closes used in meal-leg valuation
ZS closes used in soybean input-cost valuation

notes: Board-crush math follows the repo-standard beans, meal, and oil conversion formula.
Oil-share drift remains the faster watch variable than crush level by itself.

china_tension.headline: CNY is 6.8043, still 0.1957 away from 7.00, and there are zero China-tagged soybean-oil news rows in the active tape, so flow stress remains background watch rather than live rerouting.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: The China-flow score is 37.3. FX is stable-to-firm and the narrative layer is still empty on China-tagged soy pressure, so there is no evidence of an active demand-diversion shock.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This channel sits below the volatility and policy lanes, which keeps it on the dashboard but out of lead-driver status.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: China matters when it changes who clears the marginal vegetable-oil barrel equivalent. That transmission is not visible in the current tape.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: If tariff rhetoric accelerates further, this channel can move quickly, but currency alone is still not confirming a trade reroute.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: Bullish soybean-oil positioning still shortens reaction time if China headlines return, which is why the card stays alive even with zero tag confirmation.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Maintain watch status unless CNY weakens materially or China-tagged trade and soy headlines start clustering across multiple sessions.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris does not need defensive urgency here today, but he should keep this channel on the watchlist because it can move from quiet to expensive quickly.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: China Demand Rerouting and Currency Stress
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Distinguish harmless FX drift from procurement-relevant China-flow stress before the market prices in a true rerouting event.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Currency stress only matters for soybean-oil buyers when it survives multiple sessions and aligns with trade-policy pressure in the soy complex.
quantResearchProtocol: Track CNY level versus the 7.00 anchor and measure five-session change.
Cross-check FX movement against China-tagged narrative density.
Require FX and narrative agreement before escalating the channel.
Translate confirmed stress into buyer reaction-time guidance, not macro theater.
inferenceConstraints: Do not declare a rerouting regime from currency alone.
Do not overstate the channel when China-tagged narrative density is zero.
Do not use generic China-risk wording without specifying the transmission path.
outputRequirements: Quote the currency level and regime classification.
State whether the risk is flow competition, policy escalation, or neither.
Explain the procurement timing consequence in buyer language.

sourceRecords.recordHint: CNY daily close used for flow-sensitivity read

notes: China-flow risk is elevated only when FX and narrative density align.
Current tape does not show China-led confirmation, so the posture remains watchful rather than defensive.

tariff_threat.headline: Economic policy uncertainty jumped to 685.18 on 2026-05-17, and the soy-complex tape still carries 2 tariff-coded rows plus 7 broader policy rows, so policy shock has retaken lead-driver status.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Tariff and macro-policy pressure surged to 76.7 because the uncertainty index spiked to 685.18. Item density is still concentrated, but the macro series is now too high to leave policy in a background role.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This sits 35.4 points above the 41.3 average network pressure, making policy the dominant channel in today's stack.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: Policy matters here through biofuel economics, trade lanes, and replacement-cost psychology. The issue is not a physical shortage today; it is that cost expectations can gap higher before physical data move.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: The tape is still item-light relative to the size of the uncertainty spike, which means the regime is macro-shock heavy and headline-confirmation light.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: With managed money still net long 158,102 contracts, a policy surprise can propagate faster into buyer anxiety than a neutral positioning backdrop would allow.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: Escalate from elevated risk to contingency-first only if the uncertainty spike persists and starts confirming with higher volatility or additional trade-policy clustering.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris should keep buying in tranches, but the review loop needs to tighten. Policy is now the main reason not to wait passively for a cleaner print.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Macro Policy Shock and Geopolitical Escalation
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Determine whether policy uncertainty has crossed the threshold where procurement needs contingency-first execution rather than schedule-first execution.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Policy risk becomes procurement-relevant when uncertainty persists and co-confirms with energy or volatility transmission, not when it appears as a thin standalone headline pulse.
quantResearchProtocol: Track the policy-uncertainty index level and short-window change.
Count tariff- and policy-tagged soy-complex rows in the active window.
Require cross-channel confirmation from energy or volatility before calling a shock regime.
Translate confirmed escalation into optionality-preservation guidance for buyers.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call a shock regime from one data source or one thin headline cluster.
Do not minimize policy risk just because price action is calm.
Do not use vague geopolitical language without a buyer consequence.
outputRequirements: State the uncertainty level and what it implies today.
Provide the buyer posture consequence if escalation occurs.

sourceRecords.recordHint: Economic Policy Uncertainty Index latest reading
active tariff-tagged and policy-tagged soybean-oil rows
Farm, Food, and National Security Act watch item

notes: Policy pressure is treated as lead-driver only when the macro uncertainty series and soy-complex narrative counts reinforce each other.
The current regime is elevated because the series shock is large even though the item tape is still concentrated.

energy_stress.headline: Crude settled at 103.65 after only a +1.44% five-session move, so energy remains a pass-through watch channel but not the pacing driver while policy shock dominates.

whatsHappening.whatsHappening: Energy stress cooled to 29.6. CL is 103.65 and up only +1.44% over five sessions, which is firm enough to stay on the board but not strong enough to outrank policy or volatility.
whatsHappening.macroContext: This sits 11.7 points below the 41.3 average pressure stack, so energy is now context rather than command.
whatsHappening.supplyDemand: This remains a pass-through channel, not proof of a soybean-oil shortage. The issue is replacement-cost psychology, not physical shortage evidence.
whatsHappening.geopolitical: Energy can still gap on geopolitical headlines, but the current move is too contained to dominate the buyer posture on its own.
whatsHappening.investorSentiment: Bullish soybean-oil positioning can still amplify energy-led cost anxiety, but today that effect is secondary to the policy uncertainty spike.
whatsHappening.nearTermOutlook: If crude re-accelerates while OVX stays above 70, this card can climb quickly. Today it remains a watch channel.
whatsHappening.zlImplication: Chris should keep energy in the replacement-cost checklist, but it is no longer the main reason to accelerate buys.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Energy Pass-Through and Buyer Cost Shock
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Measure when crude and energy-volatility pressure are strong enough to alter soybean-oil procurement timing even without a direct soy supply shock.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Buyers often feel crude-led cost anxiety before soybean-oil physical tightness is visible, so energy can dominate procurement pacing even in otherwise moderate risk networks.
quantResearchProtocol: Track crude level, five-session change, and OVX together.
Treat positive crude momentum with elevated OVX as pass-through pressure, not just energy noise.
Compare the energy score against the full network average to measure how dominant it is.
Express the result as timing-risk for a buyer rather than as a trader signal.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call an energy-led crisis without explaining the pass-through path.
Do not ignore crude momentum just because soybean-oil spot is not exploding.
Do not present the card as a physical shortage thesis unless other channels confirm.
outputRequirements: Report crude price, five-session move, and OVX explicitly.
State whether energy is the lead network driver.
Give the buyer timing implication in clear operating language.

sourceRecords.recordHint: CL daily close and five-session move
current Today in Energy feed window supporting energy context

notes: Energy pass-through is ranked by momentum plus implied energy volatility, not by narrative alone.
This card remains framed from the buyer replacement-cost perspective.

intelligence.headline: ACCUMULATE WITH POLICY-SHOCK OVERRIDE WATCH
intelligence.summary: As of 2026-05-19T07:08:09-04:00, the average cross-driver pressure is 41.3. That keeps the posture below the WAIT threshold, but tariff threat now leads at 76.7 because policy uncertainty jumped to 685.18 while ZL is 75.37 and down -1.22% over five sessions.

drivers.label: Macro / Policy
drivers.detail: Uncertainty 685.18, tariff threat score 76.7, driver spread +35.4 versus network average.

drivers.label: China Flow Risk
drivers.detail: CNY 6.8043 with zero China-tagged soy rows leaves this channel on watch, not escalation.

drivers.label: Crush Economics
drivers.detail: Board crush 3.545 and oil share 52.86% still support throughput.

intelligence.tradingImplication: For a buyer, the right move is still staged accumulation, but the review loop should tighten because policy shock has replaced energy as the dominant risk channel.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Collapse the full risk network into one buyer-facing posture without losing the identity of the lead channel.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A soybean-oil buyer needs a single execution posture, but that posture is only valid if it explicitly preserves which channel is dominant and how far it sits above the network average.
quantResearchProtocol: Compute the average cross-driver pressure and the lead-driver spread over that average.
Map the posture threshold to ACCUMULATE, WAIT, or DEFER.
Check whether ZL and crude are moving together or diverging before finalizing the buyer narrative.
Summarize the result in timing-risk language, not speculative-return language.
inferenceConstraints: Do not collapse all drivers into a vague balanced-market statement.
Do not ignore a dominant lead driver just because the average stack is moderate.
Do not present the synthesis as trader advice.
outputRequirements: Quote the average pressure and the lead-driver score.
Explain why the posture is not more defensive or more aggressive.

notes: The intelligence block is generated from the same live market math used by the driver cards.
Buyer posture remains anchored to replacement-cost control rather than speculative return.
